title: Internet Protocol version 6
description: 128-bit addressing scheme designed to replace IPv4 with virtually unlimited address space.
fullExplanation: IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses written as eight groups of four hex digits (e.g. 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334). This provides 340 undecillion (3.4×10³⁸) addresses — enough for every atom on Earth. IPv6 eliminates NAT (every device gets a public IP), simplifies the header (fixed 40 bytes, no checksum, no fragmentation by routers), and adds built-in features like stateless address autoconfiguration (SLAAC) and mandatory IPsec support.

fields.name: Traffic Class
fields.description: QoS / DSCP marking (same as IPv4 DSCP+ECN)

fields.name: Flow Label
fields.description: Identifies packet flow for QoS (new in IPv6)

fields.name: Payload Length
fields.description: Length of data after the 40-byte header

fields.name: Hop Limit
fields.description: Same as IPv4 TTL — decremented at each router

addressTypes.description: Publicly routable addresses (like IPv4 public IPs)
Auto-configured, only valid on local link (not routed)
Equivalent to 0.0.0.0 — used before address assigned

keyFacts: No header checksum — upper layers handle error detection
SLAAC: device auto-configures address from router prefix + MAC (EUI-64)
ICMPv6 is mandatory and handles neighbor discovery, router discovery
Dual-stack: run IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously during transition
6to4, Teredo, ISATAP: IPv6 tunneling over IPv4 networks
As of 2024, ~45% of Google traffic is IPv6
